Creating a Projectile  
We've got our player all set up with an attack animation, so now let's give him an attack in the form of a  
projectile. Start by doing the following:  
Create a new actor and name it Player Projectile.  
Assign a new script to it and name it PlayerProjectile - don't add anything yet, we'll get there.  
Assign a model to it - I will use C:\tfb\content\models\test\arrow.mdl, but you are free to search  
around for something else that suits your fancy.  
1 of 10  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.1: Intermediate Scripting 1 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.1:_Intermediate...  
Provide a collision volume (say a 5 ft. wide sphere) in the collision tab.  
Uncheck the with other Actors' Shapes checkbox in the Physical Interaction section. This will  
prevent the projectile from having physics-based interactions with other objects, as in actually  
pushing them around.  
Live Updating Limitations  
If you've been connected and live updating throughout the documentation, you may notice that, upon  
addition of this actor, the actor doesn't show up in the running game. You may even notice a message in  
the Message Log that says you've been disconnected.  
While we can live update scripts, parameters like movement settings on actors, and placement of  
objects, we cannot update anything that involves the assignment or creation of an asset. Things you  
cannot update:  
Making a new actor.  
(Re)assigning of a model or script to an actor.  
(Re)assigning of level mesh.  
In general: if you need to click the New button (e.g. in the Actor Pane) or have open a ﬁle selection  
dialog for the operation, it cannot be live updated. When creating/assigning assets, I tend to break the  
connection to the target to avoid an unhappy connection.  
Select Reboot Target from the Target menu to reboot and disconnect the target.  
2 of 10  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.1: Intermediate Scripting 1 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.1:_Intermediate...  
Templated Objects  
At this point you could build and launch, and you'd see your projectile actor sitting there, on the ground.  
Since the player should, theoretically, be able to ﬁre as many projectiles as he wants, it doesn't make  
sense to have him manipulate this projectile actor. We're going to be cloning it, as covered below, so this  
is a case where we want the Player Projectile to be a template. Templated actors never, themselves,  
start up and have no presence in the world, perfect for our bullet.  
Select the Player Projectile and check the Template checkbox on the Actor Pane.  
Note that the name of the actor becomes italicized in the actor list to denote its templated property.  
Cloning an Object  
Now that we have a templated projectile actor, we can get our Player Actor to actually start shooting  
projectiles. We'll do that with the clone command, which replicates another object.  
Bring up the Player Actor's script for editing.  
Right after the play command inside the block of script where we check the controller's Action 1  
button, add a new clone command.  
Adjust the leftmost parameter to be the projectile actor at (ACTORS) > Player Projectile >  
<self>.  
Click Image to Expand  
Set the facing parameter to be my > orientation's > facing, so the projectile will be facing the  
same angle as the player when it is cloned.  
3 of 10  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.1: Intermediate Scripting 1 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.1:_Intermediate...  
Click Image to Expand  
The ﬁnal line should look as follows:  
In plain English, this means: create a copy of the actor, Player Projectile, at my location facing my  
facing. Take a moment to build and launch and you can see how the game behaves. You'll note that  
every time you press Action 1, a new arrow actor appears.  
Applying Velocity with Displace  
The clone part is working, but we're missing something very important for a projectile: velocity. We'll  
use a new command, displace, which has a few diﬀerent uses, but, in this case, it will be imparting  
velocity to our peojctile.  
Add a new displace command to the indentation of the clone block.  
Adjust the ﬁrst parameter, the target, to the scoped variable ^cloned's > velocity, so we aﬀect  
the velocity of the actor that was just cloned.  
Set the length parameter, which will be the impulse speed, to a reasonable amount, let's say 20  
feet/sec.  
Set the heading parameter, which will be the impulse's horizontal direction, to match the player's  
facing by setting it to my > orientation's > facing.  
Finally, set the pitch parameter to provide a nice upward kick at, say, 60 degrees. For your  
4 of 10  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.1: Intermediate Scripting 1 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.1:_Intermediate...  
information, 0 degrees would be straight ahead; 90, straight up; -90, straight down.  
Adjusting Movement Parameters  
Try using the player's attack in-game to see what happens. You'll note that the projectile has a slight  
vertical lob to it, but it doesn't really go anywhere, horizontally. This is because of the actor's very high  
deceleration.  
Select the Player Projectile actor in the Actor Panel and go to the Character Movement tab.  
The values you see will look like this:  
Note the very high values for Ground and Air Decel - 80 feet per second per second, meaning that  
your projectile will be losing most of its 20 feet/sec velocity very quickly.  
Try ﬁddling with those values to see how the bullet behaves diﬀerently. Eventually, for my example,  
I'll be setting the values to 0.00 (meaning no deceleration at all).  
Now try launching the projectile. Note how it skates along the ground after it does its lob - that is  
because it has 0 ground decel, so it will never come to a stop.  
Detecting Ground Collision  
Now we need a way of detecting that the projectile has impacted the ground, particularly since our  
projectile will never stop, but also because I'd like the bullet to explode when it hits the ground or a  
wall. We'll start by detecting the ground by checking the projectile actor's separation from it.  
Bring up the Player Projectile actor's script. If you left the script open while you were editing the  
player's script, you can switch to it with the handy built-in script switcher panel on every script  
(you may also Ctrl+Tab to switch between active script windows).  
5 of 10  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.1: Intermediate Scripting 1 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.1:_Intermediate...  
In the prescript, add a check value command and set the ﬁrst parameter to check for my >  
separation.  
Adjust the parameter after the from to check for my > ground's > <self>.  
We've just used a couple new concepts here: separation and ground, and I would like to talk about  
them, brieﬂy.  
While we've never explicitly checked distance, we have used it by setting it in, for example, the camera  
script. Separation is similar, but it accounts for the actor's collision volume as opposed to just checking  
the actor's origin, which is where the actor exists in space. We use separation here as a good, safe  
check to see if the projectile is resting on the ground. Due to the inaccuracies of physics, it's safest to  
check separation, as an actor's distance from its ground could be a really small number like 0.002 or the  
actor could be resting on the ground but not perfectly on its origin. [diagram]  
We also referenced a ground. All actors have a ground, which is a point that sits on the level mesh  
directly below an actor's origin. [diagram] We are checking the projectile's separation from its ground,  
which resolves to a value that is, in plain English how far away the actor is from the ground. When  
that number reaches 0, we can assume the projectile is on the ground.  
Removing an Object  
Now that we have a way to check if the projectile has touched the ground, we should remove the  
projectile when it's done. This is easily accomplished with the remove command.  
Add a new remove command to the true block of the check value in the projectile's script, and  
set it to remove myself.  
6 of 10  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.1: Intermediate Scripting 1 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.1:_Intermediate...  
Pretty simple! Remove will get rid of the speciﬁed object, which will cause it to deactivate (its script  
will stop running), physically disappear, and run its SHUTDOWN.  
Now, save your script and test out what happens when you ﬁre the projectile.  
Debugging With Prints  
After you live update, you're probably noticing that the projectile isn't appearing at all. "What the hell,  
Guide? Why teach me how to do the wrong thing?" Do not fret! It was all intentional, as, let's face it, you  
are not always going to have a perfectly-scripted guide to follow. You need to be able to debug your  
problems if you're not sure what's going on! One of the most useful ways to debug is with the print  
command, and the mantra I always repeat to my students when they aren't sure what's going on is add  
more prints.  
The print command is just like all our other script commands in that you can place it wherever you  
want. You can use it to print information to the target (the TV) or the host (the Message Log). Prints to  
the target are good for tracking persistent things, like checking how far away one object is from  
another, while prints to the host are good for spitting out a log of information so we can see what's  
happening over the run of our script.  
Debugging Theory  
Before we actually start using the commands, let's talk a little bit about what debugging really means.  
To me, debugging is 1) the process of veryifying assumptions and/or 2) working backwards from the  
part that is creating the undesired result, then ﬁguring out why the part is behaving as such. In this  
case, we assumed everything was ﬁne with our projectile script when we wrote it, but that's obviously  
not the case - it's not working! There are usually two methods I use to debug:  
Verifying Assumptions: I could start from the top down by verifying that everything that I think  
should be happening is really happening, starting where the projectile is being created, in the  
player.  
Working Backwards: I could also start from the bottom up by making an inference about why the  
projectile is not appearing and then testing my assumption.  
In this case, working backwards makes the most sense. My reasoning is that I just added a line to the  
projectile that says remove, and suddenly the projectile isn't appearing at all. I can infer that my recent  
change triggered the undesirable behavior. You will not always be able to make this inference, though!  
Just so you know, you could also work forwards by starting where the player clones the projectile, then  
verifying things are working all the way down the chain until you ﬁnd the breakage.  
Debugging the Projectile  
Since the issue seems to be very acute (the projectile isn't appearing at all), a print to host strikes me  
as the best tool for the job - I want to test my assumption that the newly added remove line is what's  
causing the projectile to not appear.  
Add a print command right before the remove line in the projectile's script. A text box will  
immediately pop up.  
This area allows you to specify some text that will come across with your print. Put something in  
7 of 10  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.1: Intermediate Scripting 1 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.1:_Intermediate...  
there to indicate what the print is, and press the enter key to close the text box.  
NOTE: The text box can be re-opened for editing by double-clicking the text ﬁeld, and can  
also be dismissed without saving changes by pressing escape.  
While a print can be used to print out information from the game, in this case we're only really  
interested in seeing that text come across. Set the ﬁrst parameter to null, specifying that we don't  
need any information about a game variable (e.g. myself).  
Finally, we need to tell the print to go to the host. Change the target parameter to host.  
Save the script and try ﬁring some projectiles. Watch the message log, and you should see your  
prints coming through.  
What did we prove? We have now shown that the remove line is, indeed, being executed, since our  
print is being executed as well. Now, the question is why? Lucky for us, the why is right in the script  
where we check for the actor's separation from its ground. Now, the greater why is: why is the  
projectile's separation from ground 0 right away?  
Improving The Check for Ground  
Although it seems buggy, the issue with our ground check makes sense: we're cloning the projectile at  
the player actor, who happens to be on the ground, and then immediately giving the projectile velocity.  
The projectile, for a single frame, will, indeed, be on the ground, even though it's about to start  
traveling upwards. We can resolve the issue by increasing the accuracy of our check: we'll check to see  
if the projectile has vertical velocity going upwards, in which case we can assume we just launched it.  
Add a new check value line inside the separation check.  
Set the lefthand side to check my > velocity's > vertical diﬀerence.  
8 of 10  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.1: Intermediate Scripting 1 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.1:_Intermediate...  
Click Image to Expand  
Set the equality check to check if it is <= meaning less than or equal to.  
Your ﬁnal line should read as follows.  
Now we'll want to move the remove logic into this secondary check. Moving lines of script is a lot like  
creating new ones - you can click and drag a line of script to place it somewhere else, and it will be  
placed above the line of script where the mouse is when you release the button. You can also select and  
move multiple lines of script at once for maximum eﬀiciency.  
Select the remove line by clicking the blue section that says remove - you'll want to click there  
because it won't pop up a menu like the gray parameter boxes.  
Note the darkened highlight bar that indicates the line is selected.  
Ctrl+Click the print line, next, to add it to the selection.  
Place the print and remove comands inside the new check value's block by clicking and holding  
on the yellow section of the print line and then placing them atop the ﬂow continue of the  
velocity check's block.  
9 of 10  
01/11/2016 03:25 PM  
Ch. 4.1: Intermediate Scripting 1 - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._4.1:_Intermediate...  
The ﬁnal result should look as follows.  
By nesting two check values, we're requiring both checks to be true before we do anything. In plain  
English, our script now says: if the actor is on the ground and is not moving upwards, then remove the  
actor. Try saving to live update the script and check out your now-functional projectile.  
Now we need something to shoot! Continue on to the second part of Intermediate Scripting.
